items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.q: Why is preserving white in watercolour important?
items.correct: White comes from the paper showing through - there is no true white pigment in traditional watercolour.
items.misconception: You can simply paint white over other colours later.
items.random_wrong: Because white is the most expensive colour.
items.why: Students plan to add white at the end as in opaque media. Watercolour is transparent - highlights must be PRESERVED from the start by leaving paper untouched or using masking fluid.

items.q: What were Impressionist painters primarily trying to capture?
items.correct: The fleeting effects of light and atmosphere at a particular moment.
items.misconception: Their feelings or first impressions of a scene.
items.why: Students take 'impression' as personal feeling. Impressionism was about visual perception - how light actually appears on surfaces, painted quickly outdoors before light changed.

items.q: Which photographer coined the phrase 'decisive moment'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Henri Cartier-Bresson. “Robert Capa” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which setting most directly controls depth of field?
items.why: Correct answer: Aperture. “ISO” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately what f-stop is considered a wide aperture providing shallow depth of field?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 f-stop. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
